item.title: MOTORING & ACCESSORIES | New luxury, new rules | Mazda CX-9
item.description: New Mazda CX-9 is all about design, comfort, luxury and technology which conveys a sense of strength and energy manifested in the New Mazda CX-9’s athletic stance, elongated hood and tapered roofline. The overall effect is a combination of composed powerful SUV with so many features. More confidence for more driving enjoyment
item.content:encoded:
New Mazda CX-9 is all about design, comfort, luxury and technology which conveys a sense of strength and energy manifested in the New Mazda CX-9’s athletic stance, elongated hood and tapered roofline. The overall effect is a combination of composed powerful SUV with so many features.

More confidence for more driving enjoyment

Available predictive i-Activ AWD all-wheel drive monitors everything from outside temperature to throttle input and braking patterns. Using that information, it adjusts power delivery to help give you optimal traction before your wheels can slip. Even when the weather’s perfect, i-Activ AWD helps stabilize and improve the Mazda CX-9’s overall road grip by sensing changes in steering effort and traction. Mazda Connect, Mazda’s infotainment system with Android Auto and Apple Car Play means you can connect your phone seamlessly so you can access your apps, work on the go and more via display screen, also contains USB ports. Other notable features of the Mazda CX-9 include, Lane-keep assist system, Rear & front parking sensors, Mazda Radar Cruise Control with Stop & Go

Mazda Advanced Keyless Entry and Start System, Blind spot monitoring with rear cross traffic alert, Rain sensoring wipers, LED headlights with auto on/off and auto height control and LED combination taillights, Premium leather seats, Power sliding moon roof with one-touch open feature & interior sunshade, and Bose Centerpoint 2 Surround Sound System with Audio Pilot 2 and 6 speakers. A Difference you can feel in performance, design, technology, style and control with 5 star i-Activ-sense safety technology helps alert you of hazards to avoid collisions.

Another way the Mazda CX-9 elevates the driving experience is by subduing road, wind and engine noise, used for sound-insulating glass, and installed even better seal insulation than in the previous generation. All this makes the Mazda CX-9 remarkably quiet, ensuring your time behind the wheel is one of minimal stress and maximum enjoyment.

Mazda CX-9’s are available at Niranjans Mazda Dealership in Suva,Nadi,Lautoka and Labasa.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: MOTORING & ACCESSORIES | Make our roads safer for everyone
item.description: Safe driving is more than obeying traffic laws it is a responsibility that protects lives reports demonplate.com, Every time we get behind the wheel, we share the road with other drivers, passengers, cyclists and pedestrians.
item.content:encoded:
Safe driving is more than obeying traffic laws it is a responsibility that protects lives reports demonplate.com,

Every time we get behind the wheel, we share the road with other drivers, passengers, cyclists and pedestrians.

A single careless decision can lead to serious consequences, which is why developing safe driving habits is essential for motorists of all ages.

One of the biggest dangers on the road is driver fatigue.

Driving while tired reduces concentration, slows reaction time, and increases the risk of accidents.

Before starting a long journey, drivers should make sure they are well-rested. Signs such as frequent yawning, drifting between lanes, or difficulty focusing should never be ignored.

Taking regular breaks every two hours and avoiding late-night driving can help drivers remain alert and focused throughout the trip.

Weather conditions also play a major role in road safety. Rain, fog and slippery roads reduce visibility and make it harder to control a vehicle.

In poor weather, drivers should slow down, keep a greater distance from other vehicles, and use headlights or fog lights when necessary.

Proper vehicle maintenance is equally important. Safe tyres, working brakes, and clean windshields can make a significant difference during dangerous conditions.

Maintaining a safe following distance is another simple but effective driving habit. Tailgating leaves little time to react if the vehicle ahead stops suddenly.

Experts recommend staying at least two seconds behind the car in front during normal conditions and increasing the gap in heavy rain or fog.

Keeping a safe distance gives drivers more time to respond calmly and avoid collisions.

Seat belts are one of the most effective safety features in any vehicle. Wearing a seat belt greatly reduces the risk of serious injury or death in an accident.

Regardless of how short the trip may be, every passenger should buckle up before the vehicle moves.

Safe driving is a shared responsibility. By staying alert, adjusting to weather conditions, maintaining safe distances, and wearing seat belts, drivers can greatly reduce the risk of accidents.

Small habits can save lives, making the roads safer for everyone.

Using a phone while driving drastically increases accident risk.

Speeding kills. Picture: shutterstock.com

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: PROPERTY GUIDE | Tropical beauty
item.description: WHEN done right, tropical houses embrace natural beauty, minimize energy consumption, and provide spaces that flow harmoniously with the environment. Today’s article explores five key features that make tropical house designs not just stunning, but incredibly effective in enhancing the quality of your life.
item.content:encoded:
WHEN done right, tropical houses embrace natural beauty, minimize energy consumption, and provide spaces that flow harmoniously with the environment.

Today’s article explores five key features that make tropical house designs not just stunning, but incredibly effective in enhancing the quality of your life.

At the heart of tropical house design is an emphasis on open spaces. The tropical climate encourages a relaxed, free-flowing living experience, and open-air concepts are a perfect fit.

By removing unnecessary walls and embracing minimalist layouts, tropical homes offer an expansive feel, where the indoors blend seamlessly with the outdoors.

The integration of large glass doors, open windows, and spacious patios creates continuous airflow, bringing in the fresh breeze and allowing homeowners to connect with nature.

This openness not only improves ventilation but also nurtures a sense of freedom and flexibility within the home.

One of the standout features of tropical house design is its focus on passive cooling techniques. In tropical climates like Bali, managing heat is essential, and these homes excel at keeping temperatures comfortable without relying heavily on artificial cooling.

High ceilings and large windows allow for cross ventilation, where cool air flows freely through the house, while warm air escapes through the higher openings.

Roof overhangs, pergolas, and deep balconies offer much-needed shade to protect interiors from direct sunlight.

These techniques work in harmony with the environment, ensuring a naturally cooler and more comfortable living space.

Tropical house design celebrates the beauty of natural and locally sourced materials, which are perfectly suited to the region’s climate. Materials such as bamboo, teak wood, natural stone, and rattan are commonly used, each chosen for their ability to stand up to the humid environment while maintaining aesthetic appeal.

These materials are durable and offer warmth and texture that can make a space feel inviting and grounded.

Furthermore, using local materials reduces the carbon footprint associated with transporting materials from distant locations, making the design more sustainable.

It also creates a home that blends seamlessly with the surrounding landscape.

Raising the structure of a tropical home above the ground is another crucial design element. This approach is particularly useful in areas prone to flooding or high humidity. Elevating the structure keeps it safe from water damage while providing better airflow underneath, which naturally cools the space.

By elevating the building, the home benefits from a lighter, airier feel, preventing moisture build-up in the lower areas and improving the overall comfort of the space.

In addition, the raised architecture adds a unique aesthetic quality to the design, making it visually striking and in harmony with the natural environment.

A hallmark of tropical house design is the seamless integration of landscaping with the built environment. Tropical homes often blur the line between the indoor and outdoor spaces, incorporating lush gardens, green walls, and tropical vegetation that contribute to privacy and microclimate control.

Outdoor living areas like patios, garden paths, and water features further enhance the tropical atmosphere, while also providing practical solutions for creating cooler, more comfortable environments.

Also, native plants require less maintenance, making them an ideal choice for your tropical homes.

Water features like ponds or plunge pools not only add to the beauty of the space but also create a calming ambiance, making the home feel like a sanctuary.

By removing unnecessary walls and embracing minimalist layouts, tropical homes offer an expansive feel, where the indoors blend seamlessly with the outdoors. Picture: manondesignstudio.com

Tropical house design celebrates the beauty of natural and locally sourced materials, which are perfectly suited to the region’s climate. Picture:manondesignstudio.com

Tropical homes often blur the line between the indoor and outdoor spaces, incorporating lush gardens, green walls, and tropical vegetation that contribute to privacy and microclimate control. Picture:

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: MOTORING & ACCESSORIES | Bimlesh: Living his dream behind the wheel
item.description: THE busy streets of Nausori never slows down. From the early rush of market vendors and office workers to the evening crowd returning home, the town widely known as “Suri” is always alive with movement. Amid the hustle and bustle, 47-year-old Bimlesh Kumar continues to serve the people of Nausori as a dedicated taxi driver, despite the rising fuel prices and increasing challenges of li
item.content:encoded:
THE busy streets of Nausori never slows down. From the early rush of market vendors and office workers to the evening crowd returning home, the town widely known as “Suri” is always alive with movement.

Amid the hustle and bustle, 47-year-old Bimlesh Kumar continues to serve the people of Nausori as a dedicated taxi driver, despite the rising fuel prices and increasing challenges of life.

Living along Bau Rd in Nausori, Kumar has spent the last 15-years behind the wheel, working tirelessly to earn a living and support his family.

For him, driving is more than just a job it is a passion that has shaped his life.

Nausori Town serves as a major hub for the provinces of Tailevu, Naitasiri and Rewa.

Every day, hundreds of people travel through the town for work, shopping, school, and other daily activities.

With the constant flow of passengers comes the daily struggle for taxi drivers trying to make enough money in difficult times.

Speaking during an interview, Kumar reflected on his many years in the industry.

“I have been driving taxi for almost 15 years now here in Nausori Town. There are a few old faces still driving, a few have changed base, and a few have passed away,” he said.

Although life has become harder over the years, Kumar remains grateful for what he has.

He lives with his family and says that not being married has made life a little easier to manage financially.

“I’m living with my family, not married, so it’s quite easy for me to handle life in this era.

One of the advantages of working in Nausori for many years is the strong connection he has built with regular passengers.

“It’s an advantage for me because most of my passengers know me, so anywhere I’m in town they will call for a pickup,” Kumar explained.

However, business is not as easy as it once was.

Kumar recalled the days when taxi drivers operated from the old bus stand, where getting passengers was much easier due to the busy location near supermarkets and shops.

“Before our old base was at the old bus stand. It was an easier route for us to get passengers because it was just next to the supermarket, but now we have a base here at the back, it’s quite hard for business.

His routine is simple but disciplined — a reflection of the hardworking spirit many drivers share.

“My normal routine in the morning is I wake up at 6am, get ready for my day, and by 7.30am I’m in town.

Living close to town along Bau Rd also works in his favour.

“Most times on a lucky day, I pick passengers on the way, and that’s where the hustle begins.”

While weekdays can be steady, Kumar says weekends are usually the busiest period for drivers.

“From Monday to Friday, my busy days are the weekends Friday and Saturday especially.

Sunday is also a good and chill day for doing jobs, but I usually do a half-day show, then I head home for a nice lunch and chill time,” he said with a smile.

Like many people around the world, Kumar says the rising fuel prices has greatly affected taxi drivers.

“As we all know, the fuel crisis is going on around the world, and I can say we are being affected. Doing small runs is useless because the fuel price is high.

“Long runs we can make few dollars and that can support our family.”

Despite the financial pressures, Kumar says safety has become one of the biggest concerns for drivers, especially during night time operations.

“It’s not about the money all the time it’s also about your life,” he stressed.

“We face a lot of challenges. Drunkards and drug gangs always force themselves to get into the car but have no fare. That is really dangerous. I have faced it a few times, so now I only drive in the daytime. It’s safer for me because we all want to be alive and go home to our family.”

item.title: TIMES SHOPPER | Poasa: Life is all about balance and making smart choices
item.description: FOR 46-year-old Poasa Vaileba, life is all about balance, hard work, and making smart choices for his family. Working as an IT technician while also managing a 16-acre farm in Nadroga, Vaileba proudly describes himself as a “double hustler” who believes that discipline and budgeting are the keys to surviving in today’s economy.
item.content:encoded:
FOR 46-year-old Poasa Vaileba, life is all about balance, hard work, and making smart choices for his family.

Working as an IT technician while also managing a 16-acre farm in Nadroga, Vaileba proudly describes himself as a “double hustler” who believes that discipline and budgeting are the keys to surviving in today’s economy.

Originally from Kadavu with maternal links to Navosa, Vaileba has spent more than 10-years shopping at RB Patel Supermarket in Sigatoka, a place he says continues to provide everything his family needs at affordable prices.

Speaking during an interview at the supermarket, Vaileba shared how important it is for families to shop wisely and live within their means.

“Shopping with a budget is always a good way to survive,” he said.

“Every week I usually spend around $100 or more on groceries and market shopping for my family. If you know how to plan properly, you can still manage your family well.”

For Vaileba, RB Patel has become more than just a supermarket.

It is a trusted place where he knows he can find quality products, good prices, and reliable customer service.

“For all the supermarkets here in Sigatoka, I always choose RB Patel because the prices are cheap and the customer service is great.

“It’s not a very big shop, but we can find all the things we need here, and that’s why I’m happy to shop here.”

Family remains the centre of Vaileba’s world.

Married to a woman from Navuso, he and his wife are raising two daughters while teaching them the value of healthy living and responsible spending.

“For my family, the main food I choose is always from the market.

“We buy vegetables, tavioka, dalo, and other fresh produce because fresh food helps keep us healthy.”

He added that meat is usually reserved for weekends, especially Saturdays and Sundays, while weekdays they focus more on vegetables and root crops.

According to Vaileba, keeping meals simple and healthy has helped his family save money while maintaining a balanced lifestyle.

“When I’m busy at work, my wife usually helps with the shopping,” he shared.

“We work together because family responsibilities should always be shared.”

Outside of his IT work, farming plays a major role in supporting the household. Vaileba grows dalo, tavioka, uvi and vegetables on his farmland in Nadroga and regularly sells his produce at the Sigatoka Market to earn additional income.

“I’m an IT technician on contract basis depending on job availability, but farming is my second income.

“The crops I plant help add extra cash flow for my family. Farming really helps people during difficult times.”

Vaileba believes many families can benefit from farming, especially as the cost of living continues to rise.

“We need to focus on things that can help our families survive,” he said. “Farming is hard work, but it can really help people if they stay committed.”

As someone who has spent years balancing two jobs while caring for his family, Vaileba also had a message for young people across Fiji.

“Go for your goals because there are many opportunities in life,” he encouraged.

“If you don’t put in the work, nothing will happen.

“Success only comes through hard work and determination.”

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: TIMES SHOPPER | VERE’S TAKE | Impact of shortage of fertiliser on food production
item.description: Bula vinaka shoppers, Global tensions and shipping disruptions are now affecting something many people rarely think about, which is fertiliser for our crops. While households in small scale farming use compost to improve soil quality, fertiliser is one of the most important elements of growing food for mass production. So problems in supply will eventually lead to higher food prices all around the
item.content:encoded:
Bula vinaka shoppers, Global tensions and shipping disruptions are now affecting something many people rarely think about, which is fertiliser for our crops.

While households in small scale farming use compost to improve soil quality, fertiliser is one of the most important elements of growing food for mass production. So problems in supply will eventually lead to higher food prices all around the world.

Modern farming depends heavily on fertilisers such as nitrogen, phosphate and potash to help crops grow faster to meet market demands locally and overseas for exports. These fertilisers are made using large amounts of natural gas and are shipped to farming communities in countries around the world.

Experts warn that when energy prices rise or shipping routes are disrupted, fertiliser becomes more expensive for everyone.

Farmers may then use less fertiliser or delay planting crops, which can reduce harvests and eventually push up food prices as there is reduced supply and high demand in the markets.

One major concern is the Strait of Hormuz, a narrow shipping route in the Middle East that is important not only for oil, but also for natural gas, ammonia and fertiliser products used worldwide.

Any disruption there affects global supply chains for farmers everywhere else.

According to global fertiliser company Yara International, around 500,000 tonnes of nitrogen fertiliser production has already been affected because of disruptions from the war.

The company warns this could reduce crop yields significantly if the situation continues, which is highly likely as we watch the news from our living rooms.

The United Nations Food and Agriculture Organisation has also warned that farming seasons cannot simply be delayed.

But if fertiliser does not arrive on time for the planting seasons, farmers will miss the best growing window, leading to lower food production. For Fiji and other Pacific countries that rely on imported food and farming supplies, global disruptions like these will increase the cost of vegetables, and other food items, we are buying from the supermarkets.

Experts say the situation shows how closely connected global politics, shipping, energy and food security are.

Fiji is facing growing concerns over a possible fertiliser shortage. Experts warn this would affect our agriculture sector, especially farmers, that rely on fertilisers.

In a recent opinion piece, economist Dr Sushil Sharma criticised the Coalition Government for what he described as a lack of preparation for this growing crisis.

He says there is no clear contingency plan, supply diversification strategy, or price stabilisation mechanism in place, to help us cushion such increases

Sharma warns that rising freight costs and possible fertiliser shortages could place severe pressure on families already struggling with high costs and heavy loan repayments.

He said reduced access to fertiliser would not only affect future harvests but threatens the livelihoods of families in the country.

He noted that the Persian Gulf supplies a large share of the world’s urea and ammonia exports which are key ingredients used in nitrogen fertilisers.

The Government says Cabinet has endorsed the Fuel, Energy and Fertiliser Crisis Response Plan aimed at protecting Fiji’s agricultural sector, strengthening food security, and supporting farmer livelihoods during ongoing global supply disruptions.

The Plan outlines coordinated measures to strengthen domestic food production, stabilise input costs, and reduce the agriculture sector’s reliance on imported fuel and fertilisers.

Key priority areas include food security and nutrition, energy resilience in agriculture, fertiliser supply stabilisation, transport and logistics support, and targeted reforms within the sugar sector.

Let’s hope as part of broader efforts to improve resilience, productivity and sustainable agricultural development. the measures will help mitigate rising farming costs, support smallholder farmers, stabilise food prices, protect household welfare, sustain rural incomes, and ensure continued access to affordable and nutritious food for Fijians

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: TIMES SHOPPER | Elevate your home with Fijian made furniture
item.description: PROUDLY Fijian made, this thoughtfully crafted furniture collection reflects a strong commitment to quality, durability, and timeless design. Built with locally inspired craftsmanship and carefully selected materials, each piece is designed to suit modern living while supporting local manufacturing and expertise.
item.content:encoded:
PROUDLY Fijian made, this thoughtfully crafted furniture collection reflects a strong commitment to quality, durability, and timeless design.

Built with locally inspired craftsmanship and carefully selected materials, each piece is designed to suit modern living while supporting local manufacturing and expertise.

The lounge suite exemplifies comfort and strength, making it an essential centrepiece for any home.

Fully upholstered in high-quality, hardwearing fabric, it is designed to endure daily use without compromising on style.

Its frame is constructed using treated pine timber for structural integrity, while the arms are finished with treated mahogany timber for added durability and elegance.

Generous 4-inch high-density foam seating, combined with additional cushioning on the backrest and footrest, ensures exceptional comfort—perfect for relaxing or entertaining guests.

The dining set continues the tradition of solid craftsmanship, featuring a robust mahogany timber tabletop that brings both warmth and durability to dining spaces.

Built with kiln-dried timber, it resists warping and ensures long-term stability. The chairs are designed for comfort, with high-density foam padded seats and foam-wrapped backrests that provide excellent support.

Traditional mortise and tenon joints reinforce the structure, while a smooth semi-gloss finish enhances its refined appearance, making it both practical and stylish for everyday use.

In the bedroom, the wardrobe and dresser showcase sturdy construction and functional design. Made from solid ply board with timber framing, these pieces offer reliable storage solutions while maintaining a clean and polished look.

The Dakua finish and stain polish add a natural, warm tone that complements a wide range of interiors, highlighting the authenticity of Fijian craftsmanship.

Completing the collection is the bed frame, designed with both durability and cultural character in mind.

Constructed from solid mixed timber and supported by strong mahogany legs, it provides excellent stability and longevity. Kiln-dried timber enhances resilience, while the distinctive tapa design spray introduces a unique Fijian aesthetic.

Finished with a semi-gloss coating and reinforced using mortise and tenon joints, the bed is a true blend of tradition and modern design.

With some of the best credit deals in town, Courts makes it easy for families across Fiji to enjoy the latest in home comfort and technology without stretching their budgets.

Flexible payment options open the door for more households to experience the quality and convenience Hisense brings to everyday life.

Hurry into your nearest Courts showroom to explore the new range of office furniture.

Scan the QR code for quick updates on OB roadshow locations and the latest red pen deals through our various channels!

Wide range of beds available for all, at Vision Investment.

Stylish and comfortable couch, Perfect for your home. Pictures: SUPPLIED

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: TIMES SHOPPER | Packed with fine cleaning particles
item.description: CIF Cream was created to solve a simple but frustrating problem – how to remove stubborn dirt without scratching or dulling surfaces. It’s creamy formula is packed with fine cleaning particles. Over the years, it has grown to become a household name, and is known for delivering a spotless finish around your house. What differentiates Cif Cream from other cleaning products is that it both pow
item.content:encoded:
CIF Cream was created to solve a simple but frustrating problem – how to remove stubborn dirt without scratching or dulling surfaces. It’s creamy formula is packed with fine cleaning particles. Over the years, it has grown to become a household name, and is known for delivering a spotless finish around your house.

What differentiates Cif Cream from other cleaning products is that it both powerful and gentle. It lifts grease, soap scum, and grime with ease, yet it’s safe to use on materials such as ceramic, stainless steel, enamel, and plastic. Making it a multi surface cleaner.

Part of Cif Cream’s standout is its versatility. It does not limit itself to one mess or surface material. It can be used to clean your kitchen counters, bathroom tiles, cookware, and even garden furniture.

As we move into a world that supports ecofriendly choices, Cif Cream bottle and recyclable, and the brand looks to continue to work on reducing its environmental impact.

Choosing Cif isn’t just about keeping your home clean, it’s also about supporting a company that is adopting to modern values of sustainability.

Cif is available in three different scents: Pink, Lemon & Original.

Whether it’s grease in the kitchen, soap scum in the bathroom, or grime in the oven, Cif Cream will ensure to deliver results that make cleaning easier and more satisfying. It’s a product that maintains a home’s comfort and shine, making your everyday mess manageable and leaving behind a scent of freshness.

Keep your house clean and hygienic on multiple surfaces. Pictures: SUPPLIED

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: TIMES SHOPPER | Bring the Soccer Fest Home | Invest in a Panasonic Mini LED screen
item.description: Bring the Soccer Fest Home with Panasonic Mini LED Big Screen Action. Feel every pass, cheer every goal, and bring the Fiji soccer fest atmosphere straight into your living room with Panasonic Mini LED 4K HDR Smart TVs. Built for families, fans, and big match moments, the Panasonic NX950 series delivers outstanding brightness, rich colour, sharp motion, and immersive stadium-like sound. With Mini
item.content:encoded:
Bring the Soccer Fest Home with Panasonic Mini LED Big Screen Action.

Feel every pass, cheer every goal, and bring the Fiji soccer fest atmosphere straight into your living room with Panasonic Mini LED 4K HDR Smart TVs. Built for families, fans, and big match moments, the Panasonic NX950 series delivers outstanding brightness, rich colour, sharp motion, and immersive stadium-like sound. With Mini LED display, Quantum Dot technology, 4K Ultra HD resolution, 120Hz refresh rate, Dolby Vision IQ, Dolby Atmos, and 360° Soundscape, every match feels bigger, clearer, and more exciting from the comfort of home.

For powerful family entertainment, the 65″ Panasonic Mini LED 4K HDR Smart TV is a brilliant choice for living rooms that need premium viewing without overwhelming the space. Its 4K Studio Colour Engine, 500+ local dimming zones, and 81W 2.1.2 channel speaker system bring sharp picture detail and room-filling sound for soccer, movies, gaming, and everyday streaming.

For bigger match-day energy, the 75″ Panasonic Mini LED 4K HDR Smart TV gives shoppers a wider and more cinematic screen experience. Perfect for family gatherings, it combines Google TV, Chromecast built-in, Google Assistant, HDMI 2.1, and Game Mode for smooth entertainment beyond soccer. Stream, cast, browse, and enjoy everything from live games to family movie nights with ease.

For the ultimate stadium feel at home, the 86″ Panasonic Mini LED 4K HDR Smart TVis made for serious fans who want every moment to feel larger than life. Its huge screen, Mini LED brightness, Hexa Chroma Drive, HDR10+, Dolby Vision IQ, and 360° Soundscape with Dolby Atmos create a bold, immersive experience that brings the roar of the crowd into your home.

From kick-off to the final whistle, Panasonic Mini LED TVs make every soccer moment more thrilling. Visit Narhari and upgrade your home entertainment with a screen built for sport, family, and unforgettable viewing.

📍 Available at Narhari branches nationwide: https://www.narharifiji.com/store-locator/

Suva, Lautoka, Nadi, Ba, Yadua–Sigatoka, Rakiraki, Nausori, Navua, Savusavu, Nakasi and Labasa.

item.title: TIMES SHOPPER | Kitchen hygiene: Essential tips for safe food handling.
item.description: A clean and hygienic kitchen is the foundation of healthy cooking. No matter how delicious a meal may look, poor hygiene practices can quickly turn it into a health risk. Bacteria, germs, and pests can easily spread in the kitchen if proper care is not taken during food preparation and storage. Fortunately, maintaining kitchen hygiene does not require complicated routines. Simple daily habits can
item.content:encoded:
A clean and hygienic kitchen is the foundation of healthy cooking. No matter how delicious a meal may look, poor hygiene practices can quickly turn it into a health risk. Bacteria, germs, and pests can easily spread in the kitchen if proper care is not taken during food preparation and storage. Fortunately, maintaining kitchen hygiene does not require complicated routines. Simple daily habits can make a huge difference in keeping your food safe and your family healthy.

Here are eight essential kitchen hygiene tips every home cook should follow for safer food handling and a cleaner cooking environment.

Handwashing is one of the simplest and most effective ways to prevent the spread of bacteria. Before you start cooking, always wash your hands thoroughly with soap and warm water. Germs from door handles, phones, money, or kitchen surfaces can easily transfer to food if your hands are not clean.

It is equally important to wash your hands again after handling raw meat, fish, poultry, or eggs. These foods may contain harmful bacteria such as salmonella, which can spread to vegetables, fruits, or cooked meals through cross-contamination. Proper hand hygiene is the first line of defense against foodborne illnesses.

Using the same utensils for raw and cooked foods can spread bacteria and unwanted flavors. For example, using one cutting board for raw chicken and then slicing vegetables on it without cleaning can transfer dangerous microorganisms.

Always use separate cooking spoons, knives, and cutting boards for raw and cooked ingredients whenever possible. After use, wash utensils thoroughly with hot water and dish soap before using them again. This simple practice greatly reduces the risk of contamination in the kitchen.

A clean kitchen is not only more pleasant to work in, but it also helps prevent pests and bacteria from thriving. After cooking, take the time to clean countertops, wipe spills, wash dishes, and put away leftovers.

Crumbs, food scraps, and grease buildup can attract insects such as ants, cockroaches, and food moths. Cluttered kitchens also make it easier for bacteria to spread unnoticed. By keeping your kitchen organized and tidy, you create a healthier and safer cooking environment for everyone.

Cleaning dishes and cutlery properly is essential for eliminating bacteria. Washing at high temperatures is particularly effective because heat kills many harmful microorganisms that survive regular rinsing.

Dishwashers should ideally run at a minimum of 60 degrees Celsius (140 degrees Fahrenheit). If washing by hand, use very hot water and allow items to dry completely before storing them. Paying attention to proper dishwashing methods ensures that plates, utensils, and cookware remain hygienic and safe for future use.

5. Store raw and cooked foods separately

Proper food storage is another key aspect of kitchen hygiene. Raw foods, especially meat and seafood, can release juices containing harmful bacteria. If these come into contact with cooked or ready-to-eat foods, contamination can occur.

To avoid this, store raw and cooked foods on separate shelves in the refrigerator. Keep raw meat in sealed containers or on lower shelves to prevent dripping onto other items. Organizing your refrigerator carefully helps maintain freshness and protects your meals from harmful bacteria.

6. Maintain the cold chain for frozen foods

Frozen foods must remain consistently cold to stay safe. Even slight thawing can allow bacteria to grow, especially in fish, seafood, and meat products.

When shopping, buy frozen items last and transport them home quickly. Use insulated bags if possible, particularly in warm weather. Once home, place frozen products immediately into the freezer. Maintaining the cold chain from store to storage helps preserve food quality and prevents spoilage.

7. Check food expiration dates carefully

Paying attention to expiration and best-before dates is an important part of food safety. Some dry goods like flour or rice may still be usable after the date has passed, but animal-based products require extra caution.

Milk, eggs, meat, and seafood should never be consumed if they show signs of spoilage or are past their safe consumption dates. Always inspect food for unusual smells, discoloration, or texture changes before use. When in doubt, it is safer to throw food away than risk food poisoning.

8. Keep pets away from food preparation areas

Pets are beloved members of many households, but they can carry bacteria, fur, and dirt into the kitchen. Dogs and cats should be kept away from countertops, dining tables, and food preparation areas whenever possible.

Animal hair and germs can easily contaminate dishes and ingredients, making food less hygienic. Setting clear boundaries for pets in the kitchen helps maintain cleanliness and reduces health risks.

9. Improve kitchen hygiene with antibacterial surfaces

Beyond daily cleaning habits, choosing hygienic kitchen materials can further improve food safety. Modern counter tops and cabinets are available with antibacterial or antimicrobial coatings that help reduce bacterial growth.

Although regular cleaning is still necessary, these coatings add an extra layer of protection. When selecting such products, always ensure they are food-safe and approved for kitchen use. Consulting a professional can help you choose the best hygienic surfaces for your home.

A hygienic kitchen is essential for healthy living.

By following these simple yet effective practices, you can create a safer cooking environment, protect your family from food borne illnesses, and enjoy greater peace of mind every time you prepare a meal.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: TIMES SHOPPER | A balanced way of life
item.description: BALANCING life between technology and agriculture, Poasa Vaileba has learned the value of hard work and smart budgeting. The 46-year-old, originally from Kadavu with maternal links to Navosa, Nadroga, works as an IT technician while also managing a 16-acre farm in Nadroga, where he grows dalo, tavioka and vegetables to support his family.
item.content:encoded:
BALANCING life between technology and agriculture, Poasa Vaileba has learned the value of hard work and smart budgeting.

The 46-year-old, originally from Kadavu with maternal links to Navosa, Nadroga, works as an IT technician while also managing a 16-acre farm in Nadroga, where he grows dalo, tavioka and vegetables to support his family.

For more than 10-years, Vaileba has remained a loyal customer of RB Patel Supermarket in Sigatoka, saying the store continues to provide affordable products and excellent customer service.

Speaking during an interview at the supermarket, Vaileba encouraged families to spend wisely.

“Shopping with a budget is always a good way to survive,” he said.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: TIMES SHOPPER | Nothing beats quality and variety
item.description: NOTHING beats quality and variety, says shopper Luisa Mateiyalona, who has made NewWorld Supermarket her one-stop shop for both her household and extended family needs. Originally from Kadavu, Mateiyalona has built a routine shopping at NewWorld.
item.content:encoded:
NOTHING beats quality and variety, says shopper Luisa Mateiyalona, who has made NewWorld Supermarket her one-stop shop for both her household and extended family needs.

Originally from Kadavu, Mateiyalona has built a routine shopping at NewWorld.

She describes the supermarket as a place where she feels comfortable and confident in the products she buys.

According to her, the wide range of goods available makes it unnecessary to visit multiple stores.

“We always come and do our shopping here at NewWorld, Greig St, Suva because they have a variety, when it comes to options,” she said.

“Their customer service is good, and I also feel comfortable coming to shop here.”

Her shopping is not only for her immediate family but also for relatives in Moala, Lau, where her family operates a small village shop.

“For our family that lives in the village, they always want to choose the best,” she explained.

“We don’t have to look anywhere else because NewWorld Supermarket has everything we need.”

Mateiyalona typically spends around $400 during major shopping trips, ensuring she gets the best value for a wide range of goods.

In her day-to-day life in Narere, Mateiyalona typically spends about $200 a week on groceries and household items.

“The prices are reasonable, especially for a family,” she noted.

She prefers shopping at the NewWorld branch nearby because of its convenience.

Being close to home saves her time and reduces transport costs, making her routine more efficient.

Occasionally, when she travels to Suva for other errands, she takes the opportunity to shop at the NewWorld there as well, appreciating the same consistency in quality and service.

“I can buy groceries, cleaning products, and other essentials all in one place without going over budget.

Convenience is another key factor in her choice. “We mostly do our shopping in NewWorld Narere because it’s close to our home.

“It saves me time and transport costs, and everything I need is easily accessible.” She added that trips to Suva also provide an opportunity to shop at NewWorld’s larger outlet.

“We had other things to buy in Suva, so we came and did our shopping at NewWorld, Suva.

Beyond price and convenience, Mateiyalona emphasised the importance of making informed choices while shopping.

“It’s very important to check expiry dates before buying anything.

“I also take time to read nutritional information on labels. You need to know what you’re putting into your body and what you’re feeding your family.

She believes that paying attention to product details leads to better health decisions.

“Sometimes products look good on the outside, but the information on the label tells a different story. Checking those details really matters.”

She also praised the shopping environment, describing it as clean, spacious, and welcoming.

“I really enjoy how clean the supermarket is,” she said. “It’s very spacious, so you don’t feel crowded, even when there are a lot of people shopping.

In closing, Mateiyalona encouraged others to make thoughtful shopping choices.

“My message to the public is choose the best and shop at places that offer quality goods,” she said.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: TIMES SHOPPER | Your food choices affects your focus
item.description: As we saw in our previous articles, ultra-processed foods (UPFs) are industrially produced and include a wide range of products, from sweetened fizzy drinks and chips, to store-bought confectionery, baked goods, instant noodles, nuggets, burgers or hot dogs. These are typically high-calorie foods rich in ingredients that are harmful in excess, such as salt, sugar, total, trans and saturated fats.
item.content:encoded:
As we saw in our previous articles, ultra-processed foods (UPFs) are industrially produced and include a wide range of products, from sweetened fizzy drinks and chips, to store-bought confectionery, baked goods, instant noodles, nuggets, burgers or hot dogs.

These are typically high-calorie foods rich in ingredients that are harmful in excess, such as salt, sugar, total, trans and saturated fats. Furthermore, they tend to have a high glycaemic index – which can cause sugar spikes and inflammation – and their regular consumption has been associated with a higher risk of NCDs such as hypertension, obesity, metabolic syndrome, heart disease and certain types of cancer.

However, recent studies have highlighted that UPFs might be harmful not only to our body, but also to our cognitive function. Specifically, diets high in processed foods are thought to increase our risk of dementia and cause a decline in cognitive abilities, in particular the ability to focus.

The findings of a recent study on Australians aged 40-70 concluded that even a small increase in daily UPF consumption (such as soft drinks, processed meats, salty snacks or instant noodles) can lead to a measurable decrease in attention span and higher risk of dementia.

Most concerningly, this occurs even in individuals who follow a healthy diet such as the Mediterranean diet (rich in whole grains, legumes, fruits and vegetables). So, while eating a balanced and nutritious diet is definitely preferable, this research suggests it may not be sufficient to protect your health if UPFs are consumed on a regular basis.

To summarize, a high consumption of unhealthy UPFs has been associated with a higher risk of NCDs such as hypertension, obesity, metabolic syndrome, heart disease and certain types of cancer, as well as a higher risk of dementia and cognitive decline.

How to boost your cognitive function through diet

Now that you know the potential harms of high UPF consumption, you may be wondering how to best boost your “brain power” through food. Here are the key steps.

Minimize your UPF consumption: reduce in particular the foods that are less nutritious and higher in salt (sodium), sugar, total, saturated and trans fats.

Check the ingredients carefully: for the UPFs you do eat, make sure you review the ingredients list and nutrition facts. Choose the options higher in precious nutrients (such as dietary fibre, vitamins, minerals and protein) and lower in the potentially harmful ones.

For the remaining meals, make sure you eat brain-healthy foods. These include:

Whole grains, starchy crops and legumes: for example rolled oats, brown rice, kumala, dalo, dhal, lentils, beans or chickpeas;

Fruits and vegetables: any type of produce will work, the more colourful the better;

Nuts and seeds: a daily handful of unsalted and not fried nuts or seeds;

Healthy fats: prioritize olive oil (rich in monounsaturated fats) and omega-3 fatty acids like walnuts, flax or chia seeds and fatty fish.

Far left: Make sure you eat brain-healthy foods like Kumala, dalo, dhal, lentils, beans or chickpeas.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: TIMES SHOPPER | Innovation, intelligence, and sustainability for the modern home
item.description: In a world where technology evolves faster than ever, LG continues to prove why it remains one of the most trusted and influential global brands in consumer electronics. Renowned for breakthroughs in OLED displays, AI-integrated appliances, and seamless smart connectivity, LG stands as a symbol of forward-thinking design and long-term reliability. At Courts, consumers can explore a full range of L
item.content:encoded:
In a world where technology evolves faster than ever, LG continues to prove why it remains one of the most trusted and influential global brands in consumer electronics. Renowned for breakthroughs in OLED displays, AI-integrated appliances, and seamless smart connectivity, LG stands as a symbol of forward-thinking design and long-term reliability.

At Courts, consumers can explore a full range of LG innovations each engineered not just for today’s lifestyle, but for the technological future ahead.

A legacy of innovation driven by intelligence

LG’s pioneering spirit is most evident in its unwavering commitment to research and development.

Every product from televisions to whitegoods to audio solutions is engineered with the next generation of technology in mind. At the heart of this intelligent ecosystem is LG ThinQ™, an AI-powered platform that unites TVs, appliances, and audio systems into a seamlessly connected network.

With intuitive mobile app controls and simple voice commands, LG ThinQ™ transforms home management into a smooth, effortless experience.

Yet the innovation extends beyond convenience.

LG’s AI systems learn from user habits, optimizing performance by adjusting wash cycles, improving picture quality, or fine-tuning audio output.

This is technology that doesn’t just work it works smarter every day.

Whitegoods: Designed for performance and efficiency

LG’s whitegoods continue to set the standard for smarter living.

Energy-efficient inverter technology powers their refrigerators, washing machines, and air conditioners, delivering long-term savings without compromising performance.

Hygiene and cleanliness are elevated through features like Steam+™, UVnano™, and auto-cleaning functions. Meanwhile, modern households appreciate LG’s sleek, ergonomic designs and intuitive controls, which make everyday tasks simpler and more enjoyable.

With high-quality components built for durability, LG appliances effortlessly keep up with busy lifestyles.

Televisions: Redefining the home entertainment experience

When it comes to home entertainment, LG remains a global benchmark. Its QNED Mini LED displays offer unmatched picture quality deeper blacks, brighter highlights, and vibrant colours that deliver breathtaking realism.

This visual mastery is powered by the α9 AI Processor, which automatically enhances clarity, depth, and colour precision.

LG’s WebOS Smart Platform further personalizes the viewing experience with tailored content recommendations and intuitive access to streaming apps.

The result: entertainment that feels immersive, intelligent, and uniquely attuned to each viewer’s preferences.

LG’s partnership with Meridian Audio elevates its soundbars and speaker systems to world-class standards. Technologies like Dolby Atmos and DTS:X transform living rooms into cinematic soundscapes, while seamless TV pairing and multi-room audio integration create a cohesive, deeply immersive entertainment environment.

A global innovation leader with local Impact

With its strong global presence and loyal customer base, LG continues to shape the future of home technology through sustainable practices from incorporating eco-friendly materials to designing energy-efficient systems and durable, recyclable products. In Fiji, LG’s presence is strengthened through Courts, the authorized distributor and aftersales provider.

Every LG product comes with a 24-month supplier warranty, and customers can enjoy Courts credit facilities and extended warranty options, making innovation more accessible and reassuring.

Whether transforming the living room with breathtaking visuals, simplifying household chores through AI-driven appliances, or upgrading sound systems with high-fidelity audio, LG consistently delivers products that enhance modern life reliably, intelligently, and responsibly.

Discover the future of home innovation today at Courts.

With LG, technology doesn’t just meet expectations it exceeds them every time.

Follow our Facebook page and Viber group for quick updates on OB roadshow locations and the latest red pen deals.

LG’s pioneering spirit is most evident in its unwavering commitment to research and development. Pictures: SUPPLIED

LG’s partnership with Meridian Audio elevates its soundbars and speaker systems to world-class standards.

Modern households appreciate LG’s sleek, ergonomic designs and intuitive controls. Picture: MELI NAGUSUDRAU

item.title: A household name in Fiji | The Motibhai Group
item.description: Over the past 90 years Motibhai has become a household name in Fiji. Specializing in wholesaling, retail, manufacturing, distribution, real estate, media and hospitality. The story began in 1929 when late Mr. Motibhai Becharbhai Patel embarked on a journey from Bombay, India to Fiji. At the age of 24, Mr Patel initially worked for a Ba merchant until he ventured out to establish his own business.
item.content:encoded:
Over the past 90 years Motibhai has become a household name in Fiji. Specializing in wholesaling, retail, manufacturing, distribution, real estate, media and hospitality.

The story began in 1929 when late Mr. Motibhai Becharbhai Patel embarked on a journey from Bombay, India to Fiji. At the age of 24, Mr Patel initially worked for a Ba merchant until he ventured out to establish his own business.

In 1931 he opened a small grocery store in Kumkum, Ba and within a short time he gained loyal customers. In 1971, Motibhai group entered the retail business, acquiring Prouds retail in Fiji, and has now expanded internationally, opening stores in Papua New Guinea.

In 1982, Motibhai further diversified by acquiring Fiji Foods and becoming one of Fiji’s largest manufacturers and producing quality products such as Golden margarine and Golden range of spices.

In 1999, Motibhai began producing what is now one of the most popular soft drinks in the country.

Sprint, which is known for its refreshing taste and delectable flavours.

In September 2010, Motibhai further diversified and entered the media industry by acquiring The Fiji Times to become fully local owned.

Later in 2014, the Group became master franchisees of the international chain Burger King, entering the quick service restaurant business.

Motibhai’ s success is not happenstance. It is rooted in the founding virtues and vision of our founders nurtured through relationships with customers, stakeholders, employees and communities.

Today, 95 years later, Motibhai Group is a diversified corporate organisation with a wide range of portfolios, and producing goods for the people of Fiji, the Pacific and beyond.

Sharon Sakonawai of Cunningham at RB Supermarket Centre Point.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: TIMES SHOPPER | Mateiyalona aims for quality
item.description: LUISA Mateiyalona believes that when it comes to shopping for her family, nothing beats quality and variety. For her, NewWorld Supermarket has become more than just a place to buy groceries it is a reliable, one-stop shop that meets both her daily needs and the expectations of her extended family back in the village.
item.content:encoded:
LUISA Mateiyalona believes that when it comes to shopping for her family, nothing beats quality and variety.

For her, NewWorld Supermarket has become more than just a place to buy groceries it is a reliable, one-stop shop that meets both her daily needs and the expectations of her extended family back in the village.

Originally from Kadavu and now living in Narere, Mateiyalona has built a routine around shopping at NewWorld.

She describes the supermarket as a place where she feels comfortable and confident in the products she purchases.

“We always come and do our shopping here at NewWorld because they have all the varieties,” she said.

“Their customer service is good, and I also feel comfortable coming to shop here.

“She also praised the shopping environment, describing it as clean, spacious, and welcoming.

“I really enjoy how clean the supermarket is,” she said.

“It’s very spacious, so you don’t feel crowded, even when there are a lot of people shopping.”

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: TIMES SHOPPER | VERE’S TAKE | Fiji faces cost of living crisis
item.description: Bula vinaka shoppers, A recent academic feature has reignited an important national conversation: Fiji is not just dealing with high prices — it is facing a full-scale cost-of-living crisis. According to a Dialogue Fiji survey, of 1,266 people in March–April 2026 nearly half of respondents see cost of living as the biggest issue, more than half are feeling pressure from rising food prices, and alm
item.content:encoded:
Bula vinaka shoppers, A recent academic feature has reignited an important national conversation: Fiji is not just dealing with high prices — it is facing a full-scale cost-of-living crisis.

According to a Dialogue Fiji survey, of 1,266 people in March–April 2026 nearly half of respondents see cost of living as the biggest issue, more than half are feeling pressure from rising food prices, and almost 70 per cent describe household financial stress as severe or very severe.

Engineer and reform strategist Sunil Chand argues this is not temporary inflation, as it reflects a deeper structural problem in how Fiji’s economy is built. Many households are no longer cutting luxuries; they are cutting essentials.

With 50–70 per cent of disposable income going toward food, fuel, transport, electricity, and housing, most of which are tied to imports, Fiji is effectively importing inflation directly into our family budgets.

At the same time, government revenue is heavily dependent on VAT, fuel duties, and import-linked consumption taxes. This creates a contradiction: when prices rise, households suffer, but state revenue also increases. In other words, parts of the fiscal system benefit from the very inflation squeezing families.

This is why tax adjustments alone, such as VAT changes in recent years (from 9 to 15 per cent then 12.5 percent), have not eased pressure in any lasting way. Fuel sits at the centre of this system.

Fiji imports about 1.6–1.7 billion dollars in fuel annually, and because fuel is embedded in transport, shipping, electricity generation, food distribution, tourism, construction, and manufacturing, it becomes the first engine of inflation.

Any global oil shock quickly translates into domestic price increases. Because fuel is priced in US dollars, any weakening of the Fiji dollar amplifies the impact immediately.

This fuel dependency feeds directly into electricity costs, since around 37–50 per cent of Fiji’s power generation still relies on diesel.

When fuel prices rise, electricity tariffs, surcharges, and business operating costs all rise with it, feeding back into higher household expenses and overall cost of living pressures.

Food imports add another layer of vulnerability as Fiji imports more than $1.1 billion in food annually. While imports can be cheaper under stable global conditions, this “cheapness” hides risk.

When global supply chains are disrupted — as seen during COVID-19 and recent fuel shocks — shipping costs surge, currencies weaken, and prices become volatile. Imported food that was once affordable can quickly become expensive.

Chand also highlights how these structural issues extend into housing and urban pressure. As rural production weakened over time, people migrated to urban centres for jobs and services.

This increased demand for housing, raised rents, stretched infrastructure, and added commuting costs — all of which feed back into the cost-of-living crisis. Fuel dependency, food imports, and weak rural economies are therefore directly linked to housing stress.

Tourism, a key pillar of Fiji’s economy, is also caught in this cycle as rising fuel, food, and electricity costs increase operating expenses while making Fiji a more expensive destination. This can reduce visitor demand, weaken foreign exchange inflows, and place pressure on employment.

Looking ahead, there is concern that Fiji may begin feeling the delayed impact of global fuel price increases soon.

A 20–25 per cent rise in fuel can cascade through transport, electricity, food prices, and household budgets, increasing the overall cost of living by an estimated 5–12 per cent.

The deeper issue, however, is historical. Since economic liberalisation following the 1987 period, Fiji has shifted from a production-based economy to one driven by tourism, remittances, and imports.

Local production weakened, rural economies hollowed out, and dependence on external goods increased.

The long-term solution, according to this analysis, is not short-term subsidies or repeated tax changes.

It is producing more food locally, generating more energy domestically, rebuilding rural economies, and reducing reliance on imported essentials.

In short, Fiji’s cost-of-living crisis is not just about prices — it is about dependency, and how the economy is structured beneath them.

Let’s change that by planting our own rations wherever we can to reduce our household budgets on food.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: LOCAL GETAWAYS | A haven of luxury
item.description: Just a 45-minute drive from Suva and only minutes from Pacific Harbour, Nanuku Resort is offering Fijians a luxury local getaway experience unlike any other. Hidden across 500 hectares of tropical landscape, the boutique resort combines exclusivity, adventure, culture, and world-class dining, all within easy reach for locals seeking a relaxing escape close to home. The 37-key property, which emplo
item.content:encoded:
Just a 45-minute drive from Suva and only minutes from Pacific Harbour, Nanuku Resort is offering Fijians a luxury local getaway experience unlike any other. Hidden across 500 hectares of tropical landscape, the boutique resort combines exclusivity, adventure, culture, and world-class dining, all within easy reach for locals seeking a relaxing escape close to home.

The 37-key property, which employs close to 300 staff, has built a reputation internationally for its premium accommodation and personalised experiences. However, resort staff say Nanuku is equally focused on encouraging more local visitors to discover what the destination has to offer.

For Tomasi, who hails from the province of Nadroga, working at Nanuku over the past five years has been both rewarding and deeply personal.

“Nanuku is more than just a workplace for me, it has become my second home,” he said.

“The support from management, the owners, and nearby communities creates an environment that feels very welcoming.”

While many visitors are drawn to the resort’s luxury villas and beachfront setting, Nanuku’s appeal lies in the variety of experiences available. Guests can spend the day snorkelling, water biking, fishing, or kayaking, with fishing trips offering the opportunity to have freshly caught seafood prepared by the resort’s chefs. Inland adventures include waterfall hikes, mountain biking trails, and river excursions that showcase Fiji’s natural beauty beyond the coastline.

For local families and couples looking for a more cultural experience, the resort also offers traditional activities such as weaving, wood carving, and interactive cooking sessions.

“We want guests to experience the real Fiji through our traditions, stories, and food.”

Food has become a major highlight of the Nanuku experience. The resort was recently recognised at the Fiji Excellence in Tourism Awards, receiving honours for Best Food Tourism and Corporate Social Responsibility. According to staff, the culinary philosophy centres around fresh local ingredients and storytelling through food.

Signature dining experiences include traditional “waria” cooking, where ingredients such as prawns, coconut cream, and taro leaves are prepared in bamboo over an open fire. Guests can also participate in chef’s table experiences featuring dishes such as kokoda, Fiji’s version of ceviche, alongside menus that change daily depending on seasonal produce sourced from local farmers and fishermen.

“Every dish reflects the people and communities behind the ingredients,” Tomasi said. “It gives guests an authentic taste of Fiji.”

The resort is also expanding its beverage offerings through partnerships with local suppliers including Moti Bai and Henneco. These collaborations not only enhance guest experiences but also contribute to community development initiatives supported by the resort.

Although Nanuku caters largely to the luxury market, with accommodation rates varying depending on season and villa type, management says the focus goes beyond high-end travel. Instead, the goal is to create meaningful experiences for guests, especially locals looking for a convenient but unforgettable escape.

“You don’t need to leave Fiji to experience something world-class, Nanuku gives people the opportunity to relax, reconnect, and enjoy something truly special right here at home.”

Nanuku is equally focused on encouraging more local visitors to discover what the destination has to offer.

Although Nanuku caters largely to the luxury market, with accommodation rates varying depending on season and villa type.

What sets Nanuku apart is not only its scale and exclusivity but also the diversity of experiences it offers Pictures; SUPPLIED .

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: TIMES SHOPPER | For a quick treat | Sunfeast Dark Fantasy Fills
item.description: Walk down any supermarket aisle and you’ll notice how certain products catch your attention. Sunfeast Dark Fantasy Fills is one of those products. With its glossy dark wrapper and golden accents, it looks less like a biscuit packet and more like a promise of indulgence. When it comes to taste, you’ll be greeted with a crisp shell that quickly gives way to melted chocolate in the centre
item.content:encoded:
Walk down any supermarket aisle and you’ll notice how certain products catch your attention. Sunfeast Dark Fantasy Fills is one of those products. With its glossy dark wrapper and golden accents, it looks less like a biscuit packet and more like a promise of indulgence.

When it comes to taste, you’ll be greeted with a crisp shell that quickly gives way to melted chocolate in the centre. It’s that contrast, crunchy outside, gooey inside, that make the experience memorable. Unlike many chocolate filled biscuits that lean to sweet or artificial, this one strikes a balance. The filling is rich and slightly dark, indulgent without being overwhelming, which makes it appealing for both children and adults. Texture is another highlight. The biscuit shell is light but firm, offering a satisfying crunch, while the filling melts with each bite, almost giving the texture of a dessert rather than a cookie. The 75g pack is worth appreciating. It’s just enough to satisfy a craving without tipping into excess. In an age where portion control and calorie awareness are on people’s mind, this size is the right size to slip into your bag or keep at your desk, for those moments when you want a quick treat.

For anyone looking to turn an ordinary snack break into a special moment, Dark Fantasy is a brand you can trust on.

A product of ITC, Dark Fantasy is distributed in Fiji by Motibhai Group – Sharing quality, sharing happiness.

Living Tuiqila shops for Sunfeast Dark Fantasy Fills at CJS Nabua.

Where every bite feels like a blockbuster.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: TIMES SHOPPER | Create a stylish and comfortable home
item.description: At the heart of the living space is seating that delivers both relaxation and design appeal. Modern overseas lounge suites, particularly recliners, are engineered with ergonomic precision to provide exceptional comfort and support.
item.content:encoded:
At the heart of the living space is seating that delivers both relaxation and design appeal.

Modern overseas lounge suites, particularly recliners, are engineered with ergonomic precision to provide exceptional comfort and support.

Whether unwinding after a long day or spending quality time with loved ones, these recliners are designed to support the body while elevating relaxation.

A wide selection of premium upholstery options further enhances their appeal.

From full leather and semi-leather to PU leather, microfiber, polyester, and fabric finishes, homeowners can choose materials that align with their lifestyle and interior preferences.

These options are not only stylish but also durable and easy to maintain, ensuring long-term value with minimal effort.

Functionality is equally important. Manual recliner mechanisms allow smooth and effortless adjustment, giving users personalised comfort at any moment. Combined with sleek, modern designs, these lounge suites seamlessly complement a variety of living spaces from compact apartments to spacious family homes.

Generous seating ensures there is room for everyone, making them ideal for family living while striking the perfect balance between form and function.

Just as the living room defines relaxation, the dining area shapes connection.

More than a place to share meals, it is where families gather, conversations flow, and memories are created.

Choosing the right dining set is key to creating a welcoming and functional environment.

Today’s furniture collections offer a diverse range of dining options, including metal, wooden, and glass sets.

Whether your style leans toward contemporary minimalism, timeless classics, or bold modern aesthetics, there is a design to suit every home.

The right table and chairs can transform any dining space into a warm and inviting focal point.

Flexibility is another major advantage. Dining sets are available in various configurations, including 5-piece, 7-piece, and 9-piece options.

Smaller sets are perfect for apartments or cozy spaces, while larger sets comfortably accommodate big families and gatherings.

This versatility ensures that every household can find a solution tailored to their needs.

Durability remains a top priority. Solid wood offers long-lasting strength and classic appeal, while metal frames provide modern resilience.

Tempered glass tops add a sophisticated, contemporary touch without compromising on durability. Complementing these materials are elegant finishes in shades like brown, black, bronze, grey, and oak—allowing seamless coordination with existing décor.

Comfort completes the experience. Padded seating, supportive backrests, and well-designed chairs encourage relaxed dining, turning everyday meals into meaningful moments shared with family and friends. Importantly, creating a stylish and comfortable home is now more accessible than ever.

With some of the best credit deals available, Courts makes it easy for families across Fiji to enjoy quality furniture without stretching their budgets.

Flexible payment options open the door to modern living solutions from premium lounge suites to elegant dining sets.

Discover the latest range of overseas lounges and dining sets at your nearest Courts showroom. Experience comfort, style, and functionality designed for today’s homes.

Stay connected with Courts for updates on OB locations, red pen deals, and exclusive offers through our social media channels.

Discover the latest range of overseas lounges and dining sets at your nearest Courts showroom. Inset: Scan the QR code below to join now! Pictures: MELI NAGUSUDRAU

Comfort completes the experience. Padded seating, supportive backrests, and well-designed chairs.

Importantly, creating a stylish and comfortable home is now more accessible than ever.

Just as the living room defines relaxation, the dining area shapes connection.

Whether unwinding after a long day or spending quality time with loved ones, these recliners are designed to support the body while elevating relaxation.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: TIMES SHOPPER | Budget doubled as prices increase
item.description: While some people regard shopping as an act of buying goods to keep their households stocked, Joan Kamikamica Smith of Suva considers it a family outing to experience and enjoy different things around the city. “Shopping for me is more like a special family time where we go out together and create a healthy eating habit, teaching my toddler the different kinds and cultures of food,” sh
item.content:encoded:
While some people regard shopping as an act of buying goods to keep their households stocked, Joan Kamikamica Smith of Suva considers it a family outing to experience and enjoy different things around the city.

“Shopping for me is more like a special family time where we go out together and create a healthy eating habit, teaching my toddler the different kinds and cultures of food,” she said.

Originally from Bau Island Tailevu Ms Smith is the general manager for Fiji Fashion Week looking after international guests and logistics.

She said having accurate prices on shelves and more cashiers could improve our shopping experience. She has noticed a price increase on items as their budget has doubled from previous years. This Mother’s Day she wishes every mother figure Happy Mother’s Day.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: TIMES SHOPPER | Have more cashiers and accurate prices
item.description: While some people regard shopping as an act of buying goods and products to keep their households, kitchen and pantries updated and stocked, Joan Kamikamica Smith of Suva considers it as a family outing to experience and enjoy different things around the city. “Shopping for me is more like a special family time where we go out together and create a healthy eating habit and teaching my toddle
item.content:encoded:
While some people regard shopping as an act of buying goods and products to keep their households, kitchen and pantries updated and stocked, Joan Kamikamica Smith of Suva considers it as a family outing to experience and enjoy different things around the city.

“Shopping for me is more like a special family time where we go out together and create a healthy eating habit and teaching my toddler the different kinds and cultures of food,” she said.

“As much as I like to do things on my own because its much quicker, I prefer going shopping with my little family as my husband and I get to spend time with my toddler where he runs around the aisles and burns out his energy”.

Originally from Bau Island Tailevu Ms Smith is the General Manager for Fiji Fashion Week involved in looking after international guests and logistics.

Ms Smith stays at her family house in Suva with her husband and a 2-year-old son. She shops every week from different supermarkets including Extra Supermarket, Newworld supermarket, Cost U Less and Fresh Choice depending on what they need to buy and the sales and promotions.

She said they have a budget of $250 to $300 a week and stick to it unless there are any events or a major function.

Smith prefers shopping on Saturday mornings as its easier to get fresh products then.

She said there are no diet plans which they follow and buy groceries depending on what meals would be prepared during the week including meat, vegetables and canned foods and often stick to local products as it is much cheaper and convenient.

“We like to plan our meals ahead for the week and buy groceries accordingly, some of the top three items which I buy every week is butter, milk and biscuits or bread,” said Smith.

She said some items like vegetables has noticeably become more expensive especially when it is being sold off season.”I love anything pickled so what I do is, I buy cucumbers, onions, cabbages and tomatoes and pickle them or store them in jars to keep it for a long period when its off season,” she said.

Ms Smith highlighted that rising food prices also affects consumers and whenever a product is on sale she buys them in bulk and stocks it based on the expiry dates.

She says one of her recent products she became a fan of is instant noodles.

“After I returned to Fiji, I started liking the spicy Korean instant noodles that I always buy”.

She said one of the pantry items she never forgets to buy is salt.

Ms Smith said she would like to see Buffalo sauces back on the shelves.

“I was trying to find Buffalo hot sauces which Newworld and Extra supermarket used to sell, but it was a bit pricey. However, I don’t see it there anymore”.

She said some of the things like having accurate prices on the shelves and more cashiers could make shopping easier and faster for customers.

“We noticed that there are a lot of counters but no cashiers to cash out and sometimes the prices on the shelves do not match with the system. I constantly check the bill and screen when cashing to ensure the correct prizes are scanned,” she said. She has noticed a price increase on items as their budget has doubled from the previous years.

She added that this Mother’s Day we all need to celebrate strength, love and sacrifice of all mothers and mother figures.

“We thank you for inspiring and nurturing our future generation and Happy Mother’s Day”.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: TIMES SHOPPER | 38 years of service as a market vendor
item.description: LIFE today is not as easy as it once was, according to 66-year-old market vendor Tevita Masavo, who has spent 38 years working at the Suva Market. His story reflects the struggles faced by many hardworking individuals in Fiji, especially those who depend on small businesses and daily sales to support their families.
item.content:encoded:
LIFE today is not as easy as it once was, according to 66-year-old market vendor Tevita Masavo, who has spent 38 years working at the Suva Market.

His story reflects the struggles faced by many hardworking individuals in Fiji, especially those who depend on small businesses and daily sales to support their families.

Originally from Cicia in the Lau Islands, Masavo is one of the long-serving vendors in Suva.

For nearly four decades, he has been selling traditional root crops such as cassava, dalo, and coconuts.

Through his years of experience, he has witnessed major economic and social changes, reflecting on the past.

“It has been years selling here in the market. In my experience in the 1990s, life was really easy, but now in the 2020s, life is very hard,” said Masavo.

His words highlight the reality that many people feel today as the cost of living continues to rise. One of the clearest examples of this change is the dramatic increase in food prices.

Mr Masavo explained, “In the 1990s, one 25kg bag of cassava was about six dollars. Now it is between $70 and $100 a bag.

This sharp increase shows how inflation has affected both vendors and customers.

For sellers, higher prices mean greater risk, while for buyers, it becomes harder to afford basic food items.

As a result, many families struggle to maintain their daily needs.

In addition to rising costs, he pointed out another challenge, changing eating habits.

Many people are now turning to processed foods and fast food instead of traditional, healthier options.

“People are buying all sorts of canned foods and fast food, and they have forgotten about healthy foods.

This shift not only affects vendors’ sales but also has an impact on people’s health.

Traditional foods like cassava and dalo have long been part of Fijian culture and are known for their nutritional value.

Despite these challenges, Mr Masavo remains committed to his work. He explained that business at the market is not always consistent.

“Our busy days are Fridays and Saturdays, but from Mondays to Thursdays it can be very hard to sell,” he said.

Even so, he continues to work every day, showing determination and resilience. His attitude reflects the reality that many vendors cannot afford to stop, even when business is slow.

Masavo also believes that honesty and dedication are key to success.

“For a seller in the market, the key role is to be honest and work hard if you want to earn a living.

Over the years, he has built strong relationships with his customers, many of whom return regularly.

He takes pride in offering quality products and ensuring customer satisfaction.

“My customers are my main priority, and they come back because they trust the quality of what I sell.

As a middleman, Mr Masavo sources his goods from areas such as Nausori, Naitasiri, and Tailevu.

This allows him to provide fresh produce while also supporting local farmers.

However, he acknowledges that external factors, including global conflicts and local issues, continue to drive prices up.

“With wars around the world and the drug crisis we face in Fiji, prices are getting higher, and the government needs to look into these matters,” he said.

Mr Masavo’s story is a powerful example of perseverance in difficult times.

His experiences highlight the challenges of rising costs, changing lifestyles, and economic uncertainty.
